messages.content:
You are ML Intern, an ML engineering assistant with 18 tools for training, fine-tuning, data processing, inference, and evaluation on the Hugging Face (HF) ecosystem. Your goal is to complete what the user requested with zero errors. You are fully autonomous — research, validate, implement, and deliver results without asking for unnecessary confirmation. # Your knowledge of HF libraries is outdated You do not know current APIs for TRL, Transformers, PEFT, Trackio, or other HF libraries. Your internal knowledge WILL produce wrong imports, wrong argument names, and wrong trainer configurations. Before writing any ML implementation code, start from the literature. The parallel research sub-agents can crawl papers, read their methodology sections, trace citation graphs, and extract the exact datasets and training recipes that produced published results. This is your primary advantage — use it. Your default workflow for any ML task: 1. Find the landmark paper(s) for the task or domain 2. Crawl their citation graphs to find recent downstream work 3. Read methodology sections (not abstracts) of the most promising papers — especially recent ones with strong results, lot of citations, and publications in high-impact conferences 4. Extract the recipe: what dataset, what training method, what hyperparameters produced those results 5. Validate and use those datasets for training ``` research({"task": "Literature crawl for [task]. Start from [paper/topic]. Crawl citation graph for recent downstream papers. Read their methodology sections (3, 4, 5) — extract the exact datasets, training methods, and hyperparameters that produced their best results. Attribute every finding to a specific result (e.g. 'Dataset X + method Y → 85.3% on benchmark Z'). Also find working code examples using current TRL/Transformers APIs.", "context": "User wants to [goal]. We need the best training recipe backed by published results."}) ``` The sub-agent knows how to use github_find_examples, github_read_file, explore_hf_docs, fetch_hf_docs, hf_inspect_dataset, and hf_papers (with citation_graph, read_paper, snippet_search, find_datasets). Be specific in your task description — name anchor papers or arxiv IDs when you have them. You can also call research tools directly (explore_hf_docs, github_read_file, etc.) for quick lookups. Skip research only for trivial non-code operations. # Mistakes you WILL make without research HALLUCINATED IMPORTS: You will import from modules that were renamed or removed. Example: old TRL trainer class names, deprecated Transformers APIs, wrong trackio config field names. Fix: read a current example script first. WRONG TRAINER ARGUMENTS: You will pass configuration arguments that don't exist in current trainer versions. Fix: fetch the actual trainer/config docs via explore_hf_docs + fetch_hf_docs. WRONG DATASET FORMAT: You will assume column names without checking. Training fails with KeyError. Fix: call hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details and verify columns match the training method. DEFAULT TIMEOUT KILLS JOBS: You will leave timeout at the default 30m for training jobs. Training takes hours. The job gets killed and all progress is lost. Fix: set timeout based on model size (minimum 2h for any training). LOST MODELS: You will forget push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id in training config. Job storage is ephemeral — the filesystem is deleted when the job ends. Without push_to_hub, the trained model is permanently lost. BATCH FAILURES: You will submit all ablation/batch jobs at once without testing that one works first. All will fail for the same bug. Fix: submit ONE job first, verify it completes successfully, then submit the rest. SILENT DATASET SUBSTITUTION: When a requested dataset fails to load, you will silently switch to a different one without telling the user. Fix: if the requested dataset isn't available, tell the user and ask what to do. PREFER HUB KERNELS OVER COMPILING ATTENTION: Do NOT pip install 'flash-attn' to enable flash_attention_2 building from source can take many minutes to hours and often fails on the job's CUDA/PyTorch combo. Instead, use the HF `kernels` library (`pip install kernels`, already pulled in by recent TRL) and load a prebuilt attention kernel from the Hub via `attn_implementation`. Examples: `AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(..., attn_implementation="kernels-community/flash-attn2")`, or `kernels-community/vllm-flash-attn3`, or `kernels-community/paged-attention`. With TRL/SFT scripts you can pass `--attn_implementation kernels-community/flash-attn2` on the CLI. Search additional kernels at https://huggingface.co/models?other=kernel. Only `pip install` extra packages (and document why) when no Hub kernel covers the need. SCOPE-CHANGING FIXES: Avoid at all costs! When you hit an error (especially OOM), you will try "creative" workarounds that change what the user asked for and/or change the training task itself — switching full SFT to LoRA on OOM, reducing max_length (silently truncates training data and changes what the model learns), disabling monitoring instead of fixing it. Do not do this. Fix errors with the minimal change that preserves the user's original request and are grounded in research and examples. If the original approach genuinely cannot work, explain why and ask the user for input before changing methods, sequence length, training approach or any other part of the task. # When writing ML code Required sequence before any training/fine-tuning/inference script: 1. Use `research` tool to find working examples, read docs, and get current API patterns 2. Validate dataset: hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details to confirm column names and format 3. Validate model: hub_repo_details to confirm model exists, correct architecture/size/tokenizer Training logging: always set disable_tqdm=True, logging_strategy="steps", and logging_first_step=True in your TrainingArguments/SFTConfig so loss values are printed as plain text lines you can grep, not hidden inside tqdm progress bars. Dataset format requirements by training method: SFT: "messages", "text", or "prompt"/"completion" DPO: "prompt", "chosen", "rejected" GRPO: "prompt" # Trackio Trackio is natively integrated with Transformers Trainer and all TRL trainers — the built-in TrackioCallback handles init/log/finish. In TrainingArguments/SFTConfig/DPOConfig/GRPOConfig set: report_to="trackio" run_name="" # e.g. "sft_qwen3-4b_lr2e-5_bs128" project="" # keeps related runs grouped so you can compare them trackio_space_id="/mlintern-<8-char-id>" # creates a public dashboard Space `project` and `trackio_space_id` can also be set via TRACKIO_PROJECT / TRACKIO_SPACE_ID env vars. Alerts are how iterations decide what to change. Use trackio.alert(title, text, level) at every decision point in training. Levels: ERROR — stop and change approach (divergence, NaN, OOM) WARN — tweak hyperparameters (overfitting, early stopping, KL spike, reward collapse, slow convergence) INFO — milestones (training complete, target reached, checkpoint saved) Always include numeric values and an actionable suggestion in `text`, e.g. "loss=12.4 at step 200 — lr likely too high, try ×0.1". A future call must be able to parse it and act on it. To add alerts under Trainer/SFTTrainer/GRPOTrainer, pass a custom TrainerCallback via `callbacks=[...]` that calls trackio.alert() inside `on_log` (training metrics like loss, reward, kl) and `on_evaluate` (eval metrics — only available here, not in `on_log`). Keep each `if` simple: one metric, one threshold. Conditions stay easy to adjust between runs. Read alerts back between runs instead of parsing thousands of metric values. CLI — always use --json: trackio get alerts --project

--run --json trackio get alerts --project

--since --json # incremental polling trackio get run --project

--run --json trackio get metric --project

--run --metric --json trackio list runs --project

--json Python: api = trackio.Api(); api.alerts(

, run=, since=); api.runs(

) (each run has .name, .config, .alerts()). Drive the next config from prior alerts: diverged → lr × 0.1 overfitting → weight_decay × 10 or reduce capacity early stopping → lr × 0.5 or adjust schedule high accuracy → refine around current config Read prior config via api.runs(...).config and only mutate keys the alerts justify changing. # Data audit Before working with any dataset, audit it first. Do not assume you know what the data looks like — inspect it. Use hf_inspect_dataset to check: schema/columns, number of rows per split, value distributions for key columns, sample rows. Surface anything notable: class imbalance, missing values, unexpected formats, outliers, duplicate rows, etc. Looking at data is the best way to boost performance of any ML model plus it reduces the likelihood of failed jobs later. # When submitting a training job Before calling hf_jobs, output a pre-flight check: - Reference implementation: [which example you based this on] - Dataset format verified: [columns confirmed via hf_inspect_dataset/hub_repo_details] - push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id set - timeout: [value] (based on: [model size] on [hardware]) - Trackio monitoring included and deploying metrics to a public Space If you cannot fill in all items, stop and complete the missing steps first. For batch/ablation jobs: submit ONE job first. Check logs to confirm it starts training successfully. Only then submit the remaining jobs. Never submit all at once. Hardware sizing: 1-3B params: a10g-largex2 7-13B params: a100-large 30B+ params: l40sx4 or a100x4 70B+ params: a100x8 Note: a10g-small and a10g-large have the SAME 24GB GPU memory. The difference is CPU/RAM only. # Sandbox-first development A private cpu-basic sandbox is already available for normal code execution in each session. For non-trivial scripts, develop and test there before launching via hf_jobs: write script → pip install → test with small run using bash/read/write/edit → fix errors → launch via hf_jobs at scale Do NOT call sandbox_create before normal CPU work. Call sandbox_create only when you need GPU hardware or another non-default sandbox tier. Use GPU sandbox (t4-small minimum) when testing code that uses CUDA, bf16, or model loading. CPU sandboxes cannot test GPU code paths. # When a task has 3+ steps Use plan_tool to track progress. One task in_progress at a time. Mark completed immediately after finishing. Update frequently to show the user what you're doing. # Error recovery When something fails: - Diagnose the actual error. Read the full error message and logs. - Do not retry the exact same thing. Identify what needs to change. - If an API/import error: check documentation for the correct API. - If an OOM error: (1) reduce per_device_train_batch_size and increase gradient_accumulation_steps proportionally to keep effective batch size identical, (2) enable gradient_checkpointing=True, (3) upgrade to larger GPU (a10gx4→a100→a100x4→a100x8). Do NOT switch training methods (e.g. SFT→LoRA) or reduce max_length — those change what the user gets. If OOM happens in sandbox, create a new sandbox with larger GPU hardware. - Never change the user's requested approach (training method, dataset, model, sequence length) without explicit approval. - If a tool call fails repeatedly for the same reason: stop and try a different approach. - Never silently substitute resources (datasets, models) — tell the user if something isn't available. # Task completion Before ending your turn, verify: - Did you actually DO what the user asked, not just explain what you would do? - If something failed: did you diagnose and fix it, or at minimum explain what went wrong and ask for user input? - For training jobs: did you include a working Trackio dashboard URL? Do not stop after describing what you plan to do. Continue calling tools until the task is verifiably done. Do not mark plan tasks as completed if they failed or are only partially done. # Autonomous / headless mode When running autonomously (no human in the loop), you MUST follow these rules: NEVER respond with only text. Every response MUST include at least one tool call. If you have nothing to do, check the plan, verify outputs or plan ahead. A text-only response ends the agent loop permanently — there is no human to re-prompt you. NEVER STOP WORKING. Do NOT decide you are "done" while time remains. The human is not watching — they expect you to use the ENTIRE time budget productively. Do NOT ask "should I continue?" or "is this a good stopping point?" — there is nobody to answer. You run until the timer expires or you are manually killed. Your workflow is a loop, not a checklist. Once you have a working result, KEEP ITERATING: LOOP UNTIL TIME RUNS OUT: 1. Research the approach (read docs, find examples, check current APIs) 2. Implement the solution (write code, set up training) 3. Train and evaluate 4. Save the model to the required output location / push it to Hugging Face Hub 5. Improve: tune hyperparameters, try different data, adjust the training recipe, try a different approach entirely 6. Go to step 1 HYPERPARAMETER TUNING: Do not tune hyperparameters by hand one-at-a-time. Write a script that launches a sweep over a grid of values (learning rate, epochs, batch size, etc.) and evaluates each run automatically. One well-designed sweep script beats ten manual experiments. If you run out of ideas: go back to the literature. Crawl citation graphs deeper — find papers you haven't read yet, read their methodology sections, extract new datasets or training tricks. Look for papers that cite your current approach and improved on it. Try combining recipes from different papers. Re-read the task prompt for angles you missed. Re-read the training logs for clues. There is always a paper you haven't read yet, and it probably has a better dataset. Check the remaining time periodically with the timer command specified in the task prompt. Budget your time: reserve at least 10 minutes at the end for final evaluation and model saving. The task is NOT done until: - The required output exists (e.g. final model, metrics reached, dataset updated etc) - You have evaluated the model and confirmed it works # Communication - Be concise and direct. No filler, no restating what the user said. - One-word answers when appropriate for simple questions. - Always include direct Hub URLs when referencing models, datasets, Spaces, or jobs. - For errors: state what went wrong, why, and what you're doing to fix it. - Do not over-explain or present elaborate option menus for simple tasks. When the user's intent is clear, act on it. Present options only when there's genuine ambiguity. - Use the `notify` tool only when the user explicitly asked for out-of-band notifications or when the task clearly requires reporting to a configured messaging destination. Do not use it for routine chat updates. # Tool usage - Execute multiple independent tool calls in parallel when possible. - HF_TOKEN is automatically available in job secrets — no need to include it extra. - For training monitoring: include Trackio in the script and provide the dashboard URL. - For private/gated datasets: HF_TOKEN is needed — it's auto-loaded into job secrets. # CLI / Local mode You are running as a local CLI tool on the user's machine. There is NO sandbox — bash, read, write, and edit operate directly on the local filesystem. Working directory: /home/alex/Projects/portkit Use absolute paths or paths relative to the working directory. Do NOT use /app/ paths — that is a sandbox convention that does not apply here. The sandbox_create tool is NOT available. Run code directly with bash. [Session context: Date=08-05-2026, Time=03:43:53.119, Timezone=CEST (UTC+02:00), User=alexchapin, Tools=18]

---
GRPO Training for Minecraft Mod Conversion — Session Handoff
Goal: Run GRPO RL training on AMD RX 6600 XT (8.6 GB VRAM, gfx1032/RDNA2, ROCm 7.1) using trl GRPOTrainer for Java-to-Bedrock mod conversion.
What's Working:
- Ollama with granite-4.0-h-micro (Q6_K, 3.2B params) runs inference locally at ~58 tokens/s
- unsloth/granite-4.1-3b-GGUF is available on HuggingFace with Q4_K_M variant (2.1 GB)
- granite-4.1-3b is a newer, smaller model that should fit in VRAM
What's Broken:
1. grpo_no_unsloth.py — OOM at training step. granite-4.0-h-micro at fp16 needs ~16GB VRAM (too large). The 16GB allocation request confirms this.
2. grpo_unsloth_manual.py — Unsloth's compiled GRPOTrainer crashes with TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for -: 'int' and 'NoneType' at epsilon_high=None clipping inside grpo_compute_loss. Root cause: GRPOConfig epsilon_high defaults to None, which causes 1 - None inside compiled torch code.
3. Unsloth FastModel can load the original fp16 granite-4.0-h-micro (6.4 GB VRAM used, 2.2 GB free) but generation OOMs because MoE architecture needs more memory during inference.
4. GGUF loading directly via transformers fails — the GGUF repo has no config.json, it's pure GGUF without HuggingFace metadata.
Key Files:
- /home/alex/Projects/portkit/scripts/grpo_no_unsloth.py — fp16 path (OOM)
- /home/alex/Projects/portkit/scripts/grpo_unsloth_manual.py — Unsloth path (epsilon crash)
- /home/alex/Projects/portkit/scripts/.venv_grpo/ — ROCm Python env (torch 2.10.0, transformers 5.5.0, trl 1.3.0)
- /home/alex/Downloads/granite-4.1-3b-Q4_K_M.gguf — local GGUF file (~2.1 GB) already downloaded
- /home/alex/granite-optimizer/ — has its own venv with transformers 5.8.0, torch 2.9.1+rocm6.3
Known Solutions:
1. Use granite-4.1-3b-Q4_K_M.gguf — the user confirmed this works (it ran fine before in the optimizer sweep)
2. Use llama.cpp or llama-cpp-python to load the GGUF directly (no HuggingFace metadata needed)
3. Fix epsilon_high=None in trl GRPOTrainer if using Unsloth path
Next Steps (Priority Order):
1. Try loading /home/alex/Downloads/granite-4.1-3b-Q4_K_M.gguf with llama-cpp-python or ctransformers/llama-cpp-python ROCm build
2. If that works, wire it into the GRPO training loop (either via trl's reward model or as a custom generation model)
3. Alternatively: try the granite-4.1-3b GGUF via AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("unsloth/granite-4.1-3b-GGUF", filename="granite-4.1-3b-Q4_K_M.gguf", dtype="auto", device_map="cuda") — the dtype="auto" on the GGUF repo might work differently
4. If all GGUF approaches fail, use granite-4.0-h-micro from Ollama as reward model + separate smaller model for training
Env vars needed: HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=10.3.0, HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1
Dataset: /home/alex/Projects/portkit/ai_engine/mmsd/data/processed/validated_pairs.jsonl (1260 pairs, 90% train split)
I'll start by reading the existing scripts and understanding the dataset, then figure out the best approach to get GRPO training working on your RX 6600 XT.

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2	"""
     3	GRPO RL Training for Minecraft Java-to-Bedrock Mod Conversion
     4	Using standard transformers + trl GRPOTrainer on AMD RX 6600 XT (gfx1032/RDNA2)
     5	
     6	Usage:
     7	    HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=10.3.0 HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1 python grpo_no_unsloth.py
     8	"""
     9	
    10	import json
    11	import re
    12	import os
    13	import sys
    14	import gc
    15	from pathlib import Path
    16	from typing import List
    17	
    18	os.environ["HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION"] = "10.3.0"
    19	os.environ["HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET"] = "1"
    20	
    21	import torch
    22	import numpy as np
    23	
    24	SCRIPT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.resolve()
    25	PROJECT_ROOT = SCRIPT_DIR.parent
    26	AI_ENGINE_PATH = PROJECT_ROOT / "ai_engine"
    27	if AI_ENGINE_PATH.exists() and str(AI_ENGINE_PATH) not in sys.path:
    28	    sys.path.insert(0, str(AI_ENGINE_PATH))
    29	
    30	DATASET_PATH = PROJECT_ROOT / "ai_engine/mmsd/data/processed/validated_pairs.jsonl"
    31	
    32	def _extract_json_blocks(text: str) -> list[str]:
    33	    return re.findall(r"```json\s*(\{[^}]*(?:\{[^}]*\}[^}]*)*\})", text, re.DOTALL)
    34	
    35	def _extract_js_blocks(text: str) -> list[str]:
    36	    return re.findall(r"```(?:javascript|js)\s*([\s\S]*?)```", text)
    37	
    38	def _extract_all_sections(text: str) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    39	    sections = {"manifest": [], "js": []}
    40	    for block in _extract_json_blocks(text):
    41	        if "format_version" in block or "header" in block or "modules" in block:
    42	            sections["manifest"].append(block)
    43	    sections["js"] = _extract_js_blocks(text)
    44	    return sections
    45	
    46	def _score_structural_alignment(completion: str, reference: str) -> float:
    47	    ref_sections = _extract_all_sections(reference)
    48	    comp_sections = _extract_all_sections(completion)
    49	    score = 0.0
    50	    total_weight = 0.0
    51	
    52	    if ref_sections["manifest"]:
    53	        total_weight += 0.4
    54	        ref_manifest = ref_sections["manifest"][0] if ref_sections["manifest"] else ""
    55	        comp_manifest = comp_sections["manifest"][0] if comp_sections["manifest"] else ""
    56	
    57	        if comp_manifest:
    58	            ref_fields = set(re.findall(r'"(\w+)":', ref_manifest))
    59	            comp_fields = set(re.findall(r'"(\w+)":', comp_manifest))
    60	            field_match = len(ref_fields & comp_fields) / max(len(ref_fields | comp_fields), 1)
    61	
    62	            ref_ver = re.search(r'format_version["\s:]+([0-9.]+)', ref_manifest)
    63	            comp_ver = re.search(r'format_version["\s:]+([0-9.]+)', comp_manifest)
    64	            ver_match = 1.0 if (ref_ver and comp_ver and ref_ver.group(1) == comp_ver.group(1)) else 0.5
    65	
    66	            score += 0.4 * (0.7 * field_match + 0.3 * ver_match)
    67	
    68	    if ref_sections["js"]:
    69	        total_weight += 0.35
    70	        ref_js = " ".join(ref_sections["js"])
    71	        comp_js = " ".join(comp_sections["js"]) if comp_sections["js"] else ""
    72	
    73	        if comp_js:
    74	            ref_funcs = set(re.findall(r'function\s+(\w+)', ref_js))
    75	            comp_funcs = set(re.findall(r'function\s+(\w+)', comp_js))
    76	            func_match = len(ref_funcs & comp_funcs) / max(len(ref_funcs | comp_funcs), 1) if ref_funcs else 0
    77	
    78	            ref_controls = len(re.findall(r'\b(if|for|while|switch)\b', ref_js))
    79	            comp_controls = len(re.findall(r'\b(if|for|while|switch)\b', comp_js))
    80	            control_ratio = min(comp_controls / max(ref_controls, 1), 1.0) if ref_controls else 0.5
    81	
    82	            score += 0.35 * (0.5 * func_match + 0.5 * control_ratio)
    83	
    84	    return score / max(total_weight, 1.0) if total_weight > 0 else 0.0
    85	
    86	def _score_length_ratio(completion: str, reference: str) -> float:
    87	    ref_len = len(reference)
    88	    comp_len = len(completion)
    89	    if ref_len == 0:
    90	        return 0.0
    91	    ratio = comp_len / ref_len
    92	    return min(ratio, 2.0) / 2.0
    93	
    94	def _score_json_validity(completion: str) -> float:
    95	    for block in _extract_json_blocks(completion):
    96	        try:
    97	            json.loads(block)
    98	            return 1.0
    99	        except:
   100	            pass
   101	    return 0.0
   102	
   103	def _score_content_density(completion: str) -> float:
   104	    code_blocks = re.findall(r"```[\s\S]*?```", completion)
   105	    if not code_blocks:
   106	        return 0.0
   107	    total_chars = sum(len(b) for b in code_blocks)
   108	    return min(total_chars / 500.0, 1.0)
   109	
   110	def compute_reward(completion: str, reference: str) -> float:
   111	    completion_text = completion if isinstance(completion, str) else str(completion)
   112	    reference_text = reference if isinstance(reference, str) else str(reference)
   113	
   114	    s = _score_structural_alignment(completion_text, reference_text)
   115	    l = _score_length_ratio(completion_text, reference_text)
   116	    v = _score_json_validity(completion_text)
   117	    d = _score_content_density(completion_text)
   118	
   119	    return 0.35 * s + 0.20 * l + 0.25 * v + 0.20 * d
   120	
   121	def load_dataset(max_samples=200):
   122	    if not DATASET_PATH.exists():
   123	        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Dataset not found at {DATASET_PATH}")
   124	    pairs = []
   125	    with open(DATASET_PATH) as f:
   126	        for line in f:
   127	            if line.strip():
   128	                pairs.append(json.loads(line))
   129	    train_pairs = pairs[:int(len(pairs) * 0.9)]
   130	
   131	    def build_prompt(row: dict) -> str:
   132	        system = ("You are PortKit, an expert at converting Minecraft Java Edition mods (Forge) "
   133	                  "to Bedrock Edition Add-ons. Given a mod description and Java source code, "
   134	                  "first reason through the platform map, then produce the Bedrock Add-on implementation.")
   135	        user = (f"Mod Description: {row['instruction']}\n\nJava Source:\n{row['java_source']}\n\n"
   136	                "Convert this to a Bedrock Add-on. First explain your conversion approach, then provide the files.")
   137	        return system + "\n\n" + user
   138	
   139	    return [{"prompt": build_prompt(p), "answer": p["bedrock_source"]} for p in train_pairs[:max_samples]]
   140	
   141	def main():
   142	    print("=" * 60)
   143	    print("GRPO Training - Minecraft Mod Conversion on AMD RX 6600 XT")
   144	    print("Using standard transformers + trl GRPOTrainer")
   145	    print("=" * 60)
   146	    print(f"torch: {torch.__version__}, hip: {torch.version.hip}")
   147	    print(f"GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}")
   148	    print(f"VRAM: {torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory / 1e9:.1f} GB")
   149	
   150	    data = load_dataset(max_samples=200)
   151	    print(f"Dataset: {len(data)} samples")
   152	
   153	    print("\n[1] Loading model with standard transformers...")
   154	    from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
   155	    import peft
   156	
   157	    model_name = "ibm-granite/granite-4.0-h-micro"
   158	    max_seq_length = 1024

   163	
   164	    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(

   172	    print("\n[2] Applying LoRA adapters...")
   173	    lora_config = peft.LoraConfig(

   176	        target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
   177	        lora_dropout=0.0,
   178	        bias="none",
   179	        task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
   180	    )
   181	    model = peft.get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
   182	    model.print_trainable_parameters()
   183	
   184	    print("\n[3] Setting up trl GRPO trainer...")
   185	    from trl import GRPOConfig, GRPOTrainer
   186	    from datasets import Dataset
   187	
   188	    prompts_text = [d["prompt"] for d in data]
   189	    references_text = [d["answer"] for d in data]
   190	
   191	    num_samples = 50
   192	    max_steps = 10
   193	    num_generations = 4
   194	
   195	    print(f"  Using {num_samples} samples, {max_steps} steps, {num_generations} gens/sample")
   196	
   197	    output_dir = "./grpo_output"
   198	
   199	    training_args = GRPOConfig(
   200	        output_dir=output_dir,
   201	        per_device_train_batch_size=2,
   202	        gradient_accumulation_steps=2,
   203	        max_steps=max_steps,
   204	        num_train_epochs=1,
   205	        num_generations=num_generations,
   206	        generation_batch_size=4,
   207	        learning_rate=1e-4,
   208	        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
   209	        warmup_steps=5,
   210	        optim="adamw_torch",
   211	        adam_beta1=0.9,
   212	        adam_beta2=0.99,
   213	        weight_decay=0.01,
   214	        bf16=False,
   215	        fp16=True,
   216	        max_grad_norm=1.0,
   217	        length_column_name=None,
   218	        mask_truncated_completions=True,
   219	        reward_weights=[1.0],
   220	        beta=0.0,
   221	        epsilon=0.2,
   222	        epsilon_high=0.3,
   223	        pad_to_multiple_of=None,
   224	        log_completions=True,
   225	        num_completions_to_print=2,
   226	        logging_steps=1,
   227	        logging_first_step=True,
   228	        disable_tqdm=False,
   229	        max_completion_length=512,
   230	        temperature=0.8,
   231	        top_p=0.95,
   232	        top_k=0,
   233	        repetition_penalty=1.0,
   234	        shuffle_dataset=True,
   235	        seed=42,
   236	        report_to="none",
   237	    )
   238	
   239	    dataset_dict = {
   240	        "prompt": prompts_text[:num_samples],
   241	        "reference": references_text[:num_samples],
   242	    }
   243	    train_dataset = Dataset.from_dict(dataset_dict)
   244	    print(f"  Dataset: {len(train_dataset)} rows")
   245	
   246	    def make_reward_func(references):
   247	        def reward_fn(completions, prompts, **kwargs):
   248	            rewards = []
   249	            for c, ref in zip(completions, references):
   250	                rewards.append(compute_reward(c, ref))
   251	            return np.array(rewards, dtype=np.float32)
   252	        reward_fn.__name__ = "reward_fn"
   253	        return reward_fn
   254	
   255	    reward_fn = make_reward_func(list(train_dataset["reference"]))
   256	
   257	    print("\n[4] Initializing GRPOTrainer...")
   258	    trainer = GRPOTrainer(
   259	        model=model,
   260	        reward_funcs=reward_fn,
   261	        args=training_args,
   262	        train_dataset=train_dataset,
   263	        processing_class=tokenizer,
   264	    )
   265	    print("  GRPOTrainer instantiated")
   266	
   267	    print("\n[5] Starting GRPO training...")
   268	    print(f"  Samples: {num_samples}, Steps: {max_steps}, Gens/step: {num_generations}")
   269	    print("  --- TRAINING START ---")
   270	    trainer.train()
   271	    print("  --- TRAINING END ---")
   272	
   273	    print("\n[6] Saving model...")
   274	    trainer.save_model(f"{output_dir}/final")
   275	    tokenizer.save_pretrained(f"{output_dir}/final")
   276	    print(f"  Saved to {output_dir}/final/")
   277	
   278	    print("\n[7] Quick eval...")
   279	    sample = data[0]
   280	    msgs = [{"role": "system", "content": "You are PortKit."},
   281	           {"role": "user", "content": sample["prompt"]}]
   282	    text = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(msgs, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True)
   283	    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
   284	    with torch.no_grad():
   285	        out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=512, temperature=0.8, top_p=0.95,
   286	                             do_sample=True, pad_token_id=tokenizer.pad_token_id)
   287	    resp = tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True).split("assistant")[-1]
   288	    reward = compute_reward(resp, sample["answer"])
   289	    print(f"  Sample reward: {reward:.3f}")

   292	if __name__ == "__main__":

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2	"""
     3	GRPO RL Training for Minecraft Java-to-Bedrock Mod Conversion
     4	Using trl GRPOTrainer on AMD RX 6600 XT (gfx1032/RDNA2)
     5	
     6	Usage:
     7	    HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=10.3.0 HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1 python grpo_unsloth_manual.py
     8	"""
     9	
    10	import json
    11	import re
    12	import os
    13	import sys
    14	import gc
    15	from pathlib import Path
    16	from typing import List
    17	from functools import partial
    18	
    19	os.environ["HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION"] = "10.3.0"
    20	os.environ["HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET"] = "1"
    21	
    22	import torch
    23	import numpy as np
    24	from tqdm import tqdm
    25	
    26	SCRIPT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.resolve()
    27	PROJECT_ROOT = SCRIPT_DIR.parent
    28	AI_ENGINE_PATH = PROJECT_ROOT / "ai_engine"
    29	if AI_ENGINE_PATH.exists() and str(AI_ENGINE_PATH) not in sys.path:
    30	    sys.path.insert(0, str(AI_ENGINE_PATH))
    31	
    32	DATASET_PATH = PROJECT_ROOT / "ai_engine/mmsd/data/processed/validated_pairs.jsonl"
    33	
    34	# ==============================================================================
    35	# Reward functions (5 rubric functions)
    36	# ==============================================================================
    37	
    38	def _simple_tokenize(text: str) -> List[str]:
    39	    text = re.sub(r"```[^`]*```", "", text)
    40	    text = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", " ", text)
    41	    return [t.lower() for t in text.split() if t.strip()]
    42	
    43	
    44	def _bleu_like(reference: str, hypothesis: str) -> float:
    45	    ref_tokens = _simple_tokenize(reference)
    46	    hyp_tokens = _simple_tokenize(hypothesis)
    47	    if not hyp_tokens or not ref_tokens:
    48	        return 0.0
    49	    overlap = sum(1 for t in hyp_tokens if t in ref_tokens)
    50	    precision = overlap / len(hyp_tokens) if hyp_tokens else 0
    51	    ref_set = set(ref_tokens)
    52	    hyp_set = set(hyp_tokens)
    53	    recall = len(ref_set & hyp_set) / len(ref_set) if ref_set else 0
    54	    if precision + recall == 0:
    55	        return 0.0
    56	    return 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
    57	
    58	
    59	def _extract_manifest(text: str):
    60	    json_blocks = re.findall(r"```json\s*(.*?)\s*```", text, re.DOTALL)
    61	    for block in json_blocks:
    62	        if "format_version" in block or "header" in block:
    63	            return block.strip()
    64	    section = re.search(r"## Bedrock Add-on Output(.*?)(?:##|$)", text, re.DOTALL)
    65	    if section:
    66	        m = re.search(r'\{[^{}]*"format_version"[^{}]*\}', section.group(1), re.DOTALL)
    67	        if m:
    68	            return m.group(0)
    69	    return None
    70	
    71	
    72	def _extract_js(text: str):
    73	    js_blocks = re.findall(r"```(?:javascript|js)\s*(.*?)\s*```", text, re.DOTALL)
    74	    if js_blocks:
    75	        return max(js_blocks, key=len).strip()
    76	    scripts = re.search(r"(?:scripts|behavior_pack|content).*?\.js", text, re.DOTALL | re.IGNORECASE)
    77	    if scripts:
    78	        return text[scripts.start():min(scripts.start() + 10000, len(text))]
    79	    return None
    80	
    81	
    82	# ==============================================================================
    83	# IMPROVED REWARD FUNCTION (2026 Research: MicroCoder-GRPO, P-GRPO, F-GRPO)
    84	# ==============================================================================
    85	
    86	def _extract_json_blocks(text: str) -> list[str]:
    87	    """Extract all JSON code blocks from completion."""
    88	    return re.findall(r"```json\s*(\{[^}]*(?:\{[^}]*\}[^}]*)*\})", text, re.DOTALL)
    89	
    90	
    91	def _extract_js_blocks(text: str) -> list[str]:
    92	    """Extract all JavaScript code blocks from completion."""
    93	    return re.findall(r"```(?:javascript|js)\s*([\s\S]*?)```", text)
    94	
    95	
    96	def _extract_all_sections(text: str) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    97	    """Extract all Bedrock add-on sections from completion text."""
    98	    sections = {"manifest": [], "js": [], "other": []}
    99	    
   100	    # Extract manifest JSON blocks
   101	    for block in _extract_json_blocks(text):
   102	        if "format_version" in block or "header" in block or "modules" in block:
   103	            sections["manifest"].append(block)
   104	    
   105	    # Extract JS blocks
   106	    sections["js"] = _extract_js_blocks(text)
   107	    
   108	    return sections
   109	
   110	
   111	def _score_structural_alignment(completion: str, reference: str) -> float:
   112	    """Score how well completion mirrors the reference structure."""
   113	    ref_sections = _extract_all_sections(reference)
   114	    comp_sections = _extract_all_sections(completion)
   115	    
   116	    score = 0.0
   117	    total_weight = 0.0
   118	    
   119	    # Manifest structure matching (weight 0.4)
   120	    if ref_sections["manifest"]:
   121	        total_weight += 0.4
   122	        ref_manifest = ref_sections["manifest"][0] if ref_sections["manifest"] else ""
   123	        comp_manifest = comp_sections["manifest"][0] if comp_sections["manifest"] else ""
   124	        
   125	        if comp_manifest:
   126	            # Check required fields
   127	            required_fields = ["format_version", "header", "modules", "dependencies"]
   128	            ref_fields = set(re.findall(r'"(\w+)":', ref_manifest))
   129	            comp_fields = set(re.findall(r'"(\w+)":', comp_manifest))
   130	            field_match = len(ref_fields & comp_fields) / max(len(ref_fields | comp_fields), 1)
   131	            
   132	            # Check version format similarity
   133	            ref_ver = re.search(r'format_version["\s:]+([0-9.]+)', ref_manifest)
   134	            comp_ver = re.search(r'format_version["\s:]+([0-9.]+)', comp_manifest)
   135	            ver_match = 1.0 if (ref_ver and comp_ver and ref_ver.group(1) == comp_ver.group(1)) else 0.5
   136	            
   137	            score += 0.4 * (0.7 * field_match + 0.3 * ver_match)
   138	    
   139	    # JS code structure matching (weight 0.35)
   140	    if ref_sections["js"]:
   141	        total_weight += 0.35
   142	        ref_js = " ".join(ref_sections["js"])
   143	        comp_js = " ".join(comp_sections["js"]) if comp_sections["js"] else ""
   144	        
   145	        if comp_js:
   146	            # Check function definitions
   147	            ref_funcs = set(re.findall(r'function\s+(\w+)', ref_js))
   148	            comp_funcs = set(re.findall(r'function\s+(\w+)', comp_js))
   149	            func_match = len(ref_funcs & comp_funcs) / max(len(ref_funcs | comp_funcs), 1) if ref_funcs else 0
   150	            
   151	            # Check control structures
   152	            ref_controls = len(re.findall(r'\b(if|for|while|switch)\b', ref_js))
   153	            comp_controls = len(re.findall(r'\b(if|for|while|switch)\b', comp_js))
   154	            control_ratio = min(comp_controls / max(ref_controls, 1), 1.0) if ref_controls else 0.5
   155	            
   156	            score += 0.35 * (0.5 * func_match + 0.5 * control_ratio)
   157	    
   158	    # Normalize by actual weight used
   159	    return score / max(total_weight, 0.1)
   160	
   161	
   162	def _score_length_ratio(completion: str, reference: str) -> float:
   163	    """Score based on length ratio (not keyword overlap)."""
   164	    ref_len = len(reference.split())
   165	    comp_len = len(completion.split())
   166	    ratio = min(comp_len / max(ref_len, 1), 1.0)
   167	    
   168	    # Penalize very short or very long outputs
   169	    if ratio < 0.3:
   170	        return ratio * 0.5  # Severely under-length
   171	    elif ratio > 2.0:
   172	        return 0.5  # Over-length gets capped
   173	    return ratio
   174	
   175	
   176	def _score_json_validity(completion: str) -> float:
   177	    """Check if extracted JSON is actually parseable."""
   178	    json_blocks = _extract_json_blocks(completion)
   179	    
   180	    if not json_blocks:
   181	        return 0.0
   182	    
   183	    valid_count = 0
   184	    for block in json_blocks:
   185	        try:
   186	            json.loads(block)
   187	            valid_count += 1
   188	        except json.JSONDecodeError:
   189	            pass
   190	    
   191	    return valid_count / max(len(json_blocks), 1)
   192	
   193	
   194	def _score_content_density(completion: str) -> float:
   195	    """Measure meaningful content vs boilerplate."""
   196	    # Remove code block markers and common boilerplate
   197	    cleaned = re.sub(r"```(?:json|javascript|js)[\s\S]*?```", "", completion)
   198	    cleaned = re.sub(r"```", "", cleaned)
   199	    cleaned = re.sub(r"\b(format_version|header|modules|dependencies|function\s+)\b", "", cleaned)
   200	    cleaned = re.sub(r"[^\w\s]", " ", cleaned)
   201	    
   202	    words = [w for w in cleaned.split() if len(w) > 2]
   203	    total_words = len(completion.split())
   204	    
   205	    if total_words == 0:
   206	        return 0.0
   207	    
   208	    return len(words) / total_words
   209	
   210	
   211	def compute_reward(completion_text: str, reference: str) -> float:
   212	    """
   213	    Improved reward: Structured field matching + validity + content density.
   214	    Based on 2026 RLHF research (MicroCoder-GRPO, P-GRPO).
   215	    
   216	    Components:
   217	    - structural_alignment (0.35): Does completion have the right sections as reference?
   218	    - length_ratio (0.20): Is output length reasonable vs reference?
   219	    - json_validity (0.25): Can we parse the JSON blocks?
   220	    - content_density (0.20): Does completion have real content vs boilerplate?
   221	    """
   222	    s = _score_structural_alignment(completion_text, reference)
   223	    l = _score_length_ratio(completion_text, reference)
   224	    v = _score_json_validity(completion_text)
   225	    d = _score_content_density(completion_text)
   226	    
   227	    return 0.35 * s + 0.20 * l + 0.25 * v + 0.20 * d
   228	
   229	
   230	def reward_func(completions, reference, **kwargs):
   231	    """
   232	    Signature: (completions: List[str], reference: str, **kwargs) -> np.array
   233	    Used by trl GRPOTrainer.
   234	    """
   235	    ref = reference[0] if isinstance(reference, list) else reference
   236	    rewards = np.array([compute_reward(c, ref) for c in completions], dtype=np.float32)
   237	    
   238	    if len(rewards) > 1:
   239	        mean = rewards.mean()
   240	        std = rewards.std()
   241	        if std > 1e-6:
   242	            rewards = (rewards - mean) / std
   243	    
   244	    return rewards
   245	
   246	
   247	def grpo_reward_func(completions, prompts, **kwargs):
   248	    ref = kwargs.get("reference", [""])[0]
   249	    rewards = np.array([compute_reward(c, ref) for c in completions], dtype=np.float32)
   250	    
   251	    if len(rewards) > 1:
   252	        mean = rewards.mean()
   253	        std = rewards.std()
   254	        if std > 1e-6:
   255	            rewards = (rewards - mean) / std
   256	    
   257	    return rewards
   258	
   259	
   260	def compute_diversity_metrics(completions: List[str]) -> dict:
   261	    """
   262	    Compute diversity metrics across multiple completions.
   263	    Used for logging to detect mode collapse.
   264	    """
   265	    if len(completions) < 2:
   266	        return {"unique_ratio": 0.0, "avg_length": 0.0, "length_std": 0.0}
   267	    
   268	    lengths = [len(c) for c in completions]
   269	    # Unique n-grams (bigrams) ratio
   270	    all_bigrams = set()
   271	    for c in completions:
   272	        words = c.split()
   273	        bigrams = [tuple(words[i:i+2]) for i in range(len(words)-1)]
   274	        all_bigrams.update(bigrams)
   275	    
   276	    total_bigrams = sum(len(c.split()) - 1 for c in completions)
   277	    unique_ratio = len(all_bigrams) / max(total_bigrams, 1)
   278	    
   279	    length_std = np.std(lengths) if len(lengths) > 1 else 0.0
   280	    
   281	    return {
   282	        "unique_ratio": unique_ratio,
   283	        "avg_length": np.mean(lengths),
   284	        "length_std": length_std,
   285	        "num_completions": len(completions),
   286	        "truncated_count": sum(1 for c in completions if len(c) >= 500),
   287	    }
   288	
   289	
   290	# ==============================================================================
   291	# Dataset loading
   292	# ==============================================================================
   293	
   294	def load_dataset(max_samples=200):
   295	    if not DATASET_PATH.exists():
   296	        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Dataset not found at {DATASET_PATH}")
   297	    pairs = []
   298	    with open(DATASET_PATH) as f:
   299	        for line in f:
   300	            if line.strip():
   301	                pairs.append(json.loads(line))
   302	    train_pairs = pairs[:int(len(pairs) * 0.9)]
   303	
   304	    def build_prompt(row: dict) -> str:
   305	        system = ("You are PortKit, an expert at converting Minecraft Java Edition mods (Forge) "
   306	                  "to Bedrock Edition Add-ons. Given a mod description and Java source code, "
   307	                  "first reason through the platform map, then produce the Bedrock Add-on implementation.")
   308	        user = (f"Mod Description: {row['instruction']}\n\nJava Source:\n{row['java_source']}\n\n"
   309	                "Convert this to a Bedrock Add-on. First explain your conversion approach, then provide the files.")
   310	        return system + "\n\n" + user
   311	
   312	    return [{"prompt": build_prompt(p), "answer": p["bedrock_source"]} for p in train_pairs[:max_samples]]
   313	
   314	
   315	# ==============================================================================
   316	# Main
   317	# ==============================================================================
   318	
   319	def main():
   320	    print("=" * 60)
   321	    print("GRPO Training - Minecraft Mod Conversion on AMD RX 6600 XT")
   322	    print("Using Unsloth + trl GRPOTrainer")
   323	    print("=" * 60)
   324	    print(f"torch: {torch.__version__}, hip: {torch.version.hip}")
   325	    print(f"GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}")
   326	    print(f"VRAM: {torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory / 1e9:.1f} GB")
   327	
   328	    # Load dataset
   329	    data = load_dataset(max_samples=200)
   330	    print(f"Dataset: {len(data)} samples")
   331	
   332	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   333	    # [1] Load model with Unsloth (FastLanguageModel)
   334	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   335	    print("\n[1] Loading model with Unsloth...")
   336	    from unsloth import FastLanguageModel
   337	
   338	    model_name = "ibm-granite/granite-4.0-h-micro"
   339	    max_seq_length = 1024
   340	    load_in_4bit = False  # Use fp16 on RX 6600 XT
   341	
   342	    model, tokenizer = FastLanguageModel.from_pretrained(
   343	        model_name=model_name,
   344	        max_seq_length=max_seq_length,
   345	        dtype=torch.float16,
   346	        load_in_4bit=load_in_4bit,
   347	        device_map="cuda",
   348	    )
   349	    print(f"  Model loaded: {model_name}")
   350	
   351	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   352	    # [2] Apply LoRA adapters
   353	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   354	    print("\n[2] Applying LoRA adapters...")
   355	    model = FastLanguageModel.get_peft_model(
   356	        model,
   357	        r=8,
   358	        lora_alpha=16,
   359	        target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
   360	        lora_dropout=0.0,
   361	        bias="none",
   362	        use_gradient_checkpointing="unsloth",
   363	    )
   364	    trainable, all_p = 0, 0
   365	    for p in model.parameters():
   366	        all_p += p.numel()
   367	        if p.requires_grad:
   368	            trainable += p.numel()
   369	    print(f"  Trainable: {trainable:,} / {all_p:,} params")
   370	    print("  LoRA adapters ready")
   371	
   372	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   373	    # [3] Import trl after model is on GPU
   374	    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
   375	    print("\n[3] Setting up trl GRPO trainer...")
   376	    from trl import GRPOConfig, GRPOTrainer
   377	
   378	    # Build prompts list (messages format for chat template)
   379	    prompts_text = [d["prompt"] for d in data]
   380	    references_text = [d["answer"] for d in data]
   381	
   382	    # Use a subset for quick test
   383	    num_samples = 50
   384	    max_steps = 10

I now have all the information needed. Let me compile the comprehensive research report.

---

# Research Report: GRPOTrainer with 4-bit Quantization on AMD GPU

## ⚠️ Critical Issue: CUDA vs ROCm

Your PyTorch reports `torch 2.10.0+cu128` (CUDA build, `hip=None`). If you're actually running on an AMD RX 6600 XT, you need the ROCm build of PyTorch (`torch==2.x.x+rocm7.1`). The CUDA build will NOT work on AMD hardware. **Verify your setup first:**

```bash
python -c "import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())"
```

If this returns `False`, you have the wrong PyTorch build. Install the ROCm version.

---

## 1. GRPOTrainer with BitsAndBytesConfig 4-bit (QLoRA) — Working Pattern

The GRPOTrainer natively supports PEFT/QLoRA. Here's the verified pattern from the source code (`grpo_trainer.py` lines 347–380):

```python
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig
from trl import GRPOTrainer, GRPOConfig

# 4-bit quantization
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b",
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",  # or {"": 0} for single GPU
)

# LoRA adapters
peft_config = LoraConfig(
    r=8,              # Low rank for 8.6GB VRAM
    lora_alpha=16,
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj"],  # adjust to model
)

training_args = GRPOConfig(
    output_dir="output",
    learning_rate=1e-5,   # 10x base rate for LoRA (PEFT docs recommendation)
    per_device_train_batch_size=1,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
    num_generations=4,    # Reduced from default 8 for VRAM
    max_completion_length=256,
    bf16=True,
    beta=0.0,             # No ref model = saves ~50% VRAM
)

trainer = GRPOTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    reward_funcs=my_reward_func,  # custom function
    train_dataset=dataset,
    peft_config=peft_config,
)
trainer.train()
```

**Key QLoRA handling in GRPOTrainer (lines 372–380):** When the model is quantized, the trainer automatically converts PEFT adapter weights to bf16 (per the QLoRA paper recommendation). No manual action needed.

**PEFT + ref model (lines 353–361):** If you pass a `PeftModel` AND `beta != 0`, it creates a "ref" adapter as a copy. With `beta=0.0`, the ref model is **not loaded**, saving massive VRAM.

---

## 2. GRPOConfig Complete Parameter Reference

Source: `/trl/trainer/grpo_config.py` (trl 1.3.0)

### Key parameters with defaults:

| Parameter | Default | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| `beta` | `0.0` | KL coefficient. `0.0` = no ref model loaded (saves VRAM). DeepSeek-R1 uses `0.001` |
| `num_generations` | `8` | G per prompt. **Must divide generation_batch_size. Min 2.** |
| `num_generations_eval` | `None` | Falls back to `num_generations` |
| `max_completion_length` | `256` | Max tokens per completion |
| `generation_batch_size` | `None` | Defaults to effective batch size |
| `steps_per_generation` | `None` | Defaults to `gradient_accumulation_steps`. Mutually exclusive with `generation_batch_size` |
| `temperature` | `1.0` | Sampling temperature |
| `epsilon` | `0.2` | PPO clipping epsilon (lower bound) |
| `epsilon_high` | `None` | Upper clipping epsilon. Defaults to `epsilon`. DAPO recommends `0.28` |
| `delta` | `None` | Two-sided GRPO upper clip bound |
| `num_iterations` | `1` | Iterations per batch (μ) |
| `scale_rewards` | `"group"` | `"group"`/`True`, `"batch"`, `False`/`"none"` |
| `loss_type` | `"dapo"` | `"grpo"`, `"dapo"` (default, recommended), `"bnpo"`, `"dr_grpo"`, `"cispo"`, `"sapo"`, `"luspo"`, `"vespo"` |
| `importance_sampling_level` | `"token"` | `"token"` or `"sequence"` |
| `mask_truncated_completions` | `False` | DAPO recommends `True` |
| `reward_weights` | `None` | Per-reward-func weights |
| `multi_objective_aggregation` | `"sum_then_normalize"` | Or `"normalize_then_sum"` (GDPO) |
| `top_entropy_quantile` | `1.0` | `0.2` recommended by Beyond 80/20 paper |
| `sync_ref_model` | `False` | TR-DPO periodic sync |
| `gradient_checkpointing` | `True` | **Default ON** in GRPO |
| `learning_rate` | `1e-6` | Default for full fine-tuning. **Use `1e-5` for LoRA** (per PEFT docs) |
| `logging_steps` | `10` | Overridden from default 500 |
| `bf16` | `True` | Auto-enabled if fp16 not set |

### Overridden TrainingArguments defaults:
- `logging_steps`: 10 (not 500)
- `gradient_checkpointing`: True (not False)
- `bf16`: True if fp16 not set
- `learning_rate`: 1e-6 (not 5e-5)

---

## 3. Custom Reward Function Signature

From `grpo_trainer.py` lines 1218–1227:

```python
def my_reward_func(prompts, completions, completion_ids, **kwargs):
    """
    Args:
        prompts: list of list of dicts (conversational format)
            e.g. [[{"role": "user", "content": "Solve: 2+2"}], ...]
        completions: list of list of dicts
            e.g. [[{"role": "assistant", "content": "The answer is 4"}], ...]
        completion_ids: list of list of ints (token IDs)
        **kwargs: any extra columns from dataset, plus:
            - trainer_state: TrainerState object
            - log_extra: callable for logging extra columns
            - log_metric: callable for logging scalar metrics
    
    Returns:
        list[float]: one reward per (prompt, completion) pair
        OR list[float | None]: None means skip that sample for this reward func
    """
    rewards = []
    for completion in completions:
        content = completion[0]["content"]
        # Your reward logic here
        rewards.append(1.0 if "correct_answer" in content else 0.0)
    return rewards
```

**Critical details:**
- Custom reward functions receive `prompts`, `completions`, `completion_ids` as positional args
- **Any additional dataset columns** are passed as kwargs (so `remove_unused_columns` defaults to `False`)
- Return `None` for samples where the reward doesn't apply (useful for multi-task)
- Built-in reward functions from `trl.rewards`: `accuracy_reward`, `reasoning_accuracy_reward`, `think_format_reward`, `get_soft_overlong_punishment`
- Multiple reward functions are summed (with optional `reward_weights`)

---

## 4. Multiple Reward Functions Example

```python
from trl import GRPOTrainer, GRPOConfig
from trl.rewards import accuracy_reward, think_format_reward, get_soft_overlong_punishment

soft_punishment = get_soft_overlong_punishment(max_completion_len=256, soft_punish_cache=20)

training_args = GRPOConfig(
    reward_weights=[1.0, 0.2, 0.5],  # Optional: weight per function
    multi_objective_aggregation="sum_then_normalize",  # or "normalize_then_sum"
)

trainer = GRPOTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    reward_funcs=[accuracy_reward, think_format_reward, soft_punishment],
    train_dataset=dataset,
)
```

Note: `accuracy_reward` requires a `solution` column in dataset (passed via kwargs).

---

## 5. PEFT/LoRA + 4-bit Quantization Compatibility

**Confirmed working** in GRPOTrainer. The code path (lines 363–380):

1. `peft_config` is passed → `model = get_peft_model(model, peft_config)` (line 365)
2. If gradient checkpointing (default ON) → `model.enable_input_require_grads()` (line 370)
3. If model is quantized (`is_loaded_in_4bit`) → adapter weights auto-converted to bf16 (lines 377–380)

**For ref model with PEFT** (lines 353–361): If passing an already-PEFT-wrapped model with `beta != 0`, it creates a "ref" adapter copy. **Recommendation for 8.6GB: use `beta=0.0`** to avoid loading ref model entirely.

---

## 6. bitsandbytes on AMD ROCm — Compatibility Check

**bitsandbytes 0.49.2 has official ROCm support** (marked as "Preview"). Per the docs:

| ROCm Version | Supported Targets |
|-------------|-------------------|
| 7.1.0 | CDNA: gfx90a, gfx942, gfx950 / RDNA: gfx1100, gfx1101, gfx1150, gfx1151, gfx1200, gfx1201 |

**RX 6600 XT = gfx1032 (RDNA2).** This is **NOT in the supported target list**. The prebuilt wheels only include gfx1100/gfx1101 (RDNA3). 

**Options:**
1. **Compile from source** with `-DBNB_ROCM_ARCH="gfx1032"` — may work but untested
2. Use `bitsandbytes` CUDA build — will NOT work on AMD
3. **Consider alternative quantization**: GPTQ or AWQ (but those also have limited AMD support)
4. **Full bf16 without quantization**: granite-4.1-3b in bf16 ≈ 6.8GB, leaving ~1.8GB for activations/LoRA. With `per_device_train_batch_size=1`, `num_generations=2`, short completions, and gradient checkpointing, this is tight but potentially feasible.

---

## 7. Essential Code Patterns & References

### Key file paths (trl 1.3.0):
- Config: `trl/trainer/grpo_config.py` — all GRPOConfig field defaults
- Trainer: `trl/trainer/grpo_trainer.py` — reward_funcs handling at L382-399, reward computation at L1175-1254, PEFT/QLoRA at L347-380
- Built-in rewards: `trl/rewards/` — `accuracy_rewards.py`, `format_rewards.py`, `other_rewards.py`

### Recommended config for 8.6GB VRAM:

```python
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig
from trl import GRPOConfig, GRPOTrainer
import torch

# If bitsandbytes works on your AMD GPU:
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b",
    quantization_config=BitsAndBytesConfig(
        load_in_4bit=True,
        bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
        bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    ),
    device_map={"": 0},
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

peft_config = LoraConfig(
    r=8, lora_alpha=16, lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # Minimal targets for VRAM
)

config = GRPOConfig(
    output_dir="grpo-output",
    # Training
    learning_rate=1e-5,           # 10x base for LoRA
    per_device_train_batch_size=1,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=2,
    # Generation — minimize for VRAM
    num_generations=2,            # Minimum allowed (default 8)
    max_completion_length=128,    # Short to save VRAM
    generation_batch_size=2,      # Must be divisible by num_generations
    # GRPO specifics
    beta=0.0,                     # No ref model = big VRAM savings
    epsilon=0.2,
    loss_type="dapo",             # Default, best
    mask_truncated_completions=True,
    scale_rewards="group",
    # Memory
    bf16=True,
    gradient_checkpointing=True,  # Default ON
    max_grad_norm=1.0,
    # Logging
    logging_steps=10,
    log_completions=True,
)
```

**VRAM budget estimation (4-bit):**
- Base model 4-bit: ~1.7GB
- LoRA adapters (r=8, 2 targets): ~50MB
- Generation (2 completions × 128 tokens): ~1-2GB
- Gradients + optimizer states: ~500MB-1GB
- Activations (with gradient checkpointing): ~1-2GB
- **Total: ~4-6GB** — fits in 8.6GB

**VRAM budget estimation (bf16, no quantization):**
- Base model bf16: ~6.8GB
- LoRA + generation + activations: ~3-4GB
- **Total: ~10-11GB** — DOES NOT FIT in 8.6GB without quantization

---

## Recommendations

1. **Quantization is mandatory** for 8.6GB VRAM with a 3.4B model. bitsandbytes ROCm support for gfx1032 is not in prebuilt wheels — you'll need to compile from source with `cmake -DCOMPUTE_BACKEND=hip -DBNB_ROCM_ARCH="gfx1032"`.

2. **Use `beta=0.0`** (default) — this skips loading the reference model, cutting VRAM nearly in half.

3. **Use `num_generations=2`** (minimum) — generation is the biggest VRAM consumer during GRPO.

4. **Target minimal LoRA modules** (`q_proj`, `v_proj` only) to reduce trainable parameters.

5. **Consider a smaller model** (e.g., Qwen2.5-0.5B or 1.5B) if bitsandbytes doesn't compile for gfx1032.

messages.content: The research is extremely helpful. Key insight: **RX 6600 XT (gfx1032) is NOT in the official bitsandbytes ROCm support list**. But we already verified that `Linear4bit` creation succeeded! Let me verify this more thoroughly, then build the script:

function.arguments:
{"command":"cd /home/alex/Projects/portkit/scripts && . .venv_grpo/bin/activate && HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=10.3.0 HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1 python -c "
import os
os.environ['HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION'] = '10.3.0'
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig

print('Testing bitsandbytes 4-bit load of granite-4.1-3b...')

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.float16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b',
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map={'': 0},
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    trust_remote_code=True,
)

print(f'Model loaded: {model.__class__.__name__}')
print(f'Model device: {model.device}')
print(f'VRAM used: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
print(f'VRAM reserved: {torch.cuda.memory_reserved() / 1e9:.2f} GB')

# Test a forward pass
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b', trust_remote_code=True)
if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
inputs = tokenizer('Hello, how are you?', return_tensors='pt').to('cuda')
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=False)
print(f'Generation test: {tokenizer.decode(out[0])}')
print(f'VRAM after gen: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
print('SUCCESS: 4-bit load + generation works!')
" 2>&1","description":"Test bitsandbytes 4-bit granite-4.1-3b on ROCm","timeout":300}

print(f'Free after gen: {(torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory - torch.cuda.memory_reserved()) / 1e9:.2f} GB')

messages.content: Model loads in fp16 at 6.81 GB, leaving 1.76 GB free. Generation works fine. But 1.76 GB free won't be enough for GRPO training with gradients, optimizer states, and multiple generations

# Fine-tuning LLMs on AMD GPUs with Unsloth Guide Fine-tune LLMs up to 2x faster with \~70% less memory on AMD hardware, no NVIDIA required. Unsloth supports AMD Radeon RDNA 2/3/3.5/4 (RX 6000–9000 series) and data center GPUs including the MI300X (192GB). {% stepper %} {% step %} #### **One-line installer** **Easiest install:** Skip all the steps below with the one-line installer, it auto-detects your AMD GPU, installs ROCm-optimized PyTorch, bitsandbytes, and launches Unsloth Studio: ``` curl -fsSL https://unsloth.ai/install.sh | sh ```
The manual steps below are for users who want a Python library-only install without Studio. {% endstep %} {% step %} #### **Make a new isolated environment (Optional)** To not break any system packages, you can make an isolated pip environment. Reminder to check what Python version you have! It might be `pip3`, `pip3.13`, `python3`, `python.3.13` etc. {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```bash apt update && apt install python3.10-venv python3.11-venv python3.12-venv python3.13-venv -y python3 -m venv unsloth_env source unsloth_env/bin/activate pip install uv ``` {% endcode %}
{% endstep %} {% step %} #### **Install PyTorch** *Install PyTorch with ROCm support from* Check your ROCm version via \`amd-smi --version\` then change `https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm7.1` to match it. **ROCm 6.0 or newer is required.** ROCm 5.x and below have no PyTorch wheels. {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```bash uv pip install "torch>=2.4,<2.11.0" "torchvision<0.26.0" "torchaudio<2.11.0" \ --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm7.1 --upgrade --force-reinstall ``` {% endcode %}
*The version caps prevent accidentally pulling torch 2.11+ which only has ROCm 7.2 wheels and will break things. Update `rocm7.1` to match your detected version as before.* We also wrote a single terminal command to extract the correct ROCM version if it helps. ```bash ROCM_TAG="$({ command -v amd-smi >/dev/null 2>&1 && amd-smi version 2>/dev/null | awk -F'ROCm version: ' 'NF>1{split($2,a,"."); print "rocm"a[1]"."a[2]; ok=1; exit} END{exit !ok}'; } || { [ -r /opt/rocm/.info/version ] && awk -F. '{print "rocm"$1"."$2; exit}' /opt/rocm/.info/version; } || { command -v hipconfig >/dev/null 2>&1 && hipconfig --version 2>/dev/null | awk -F': *' '/HIP version/{split($2,a,"."); print "rocm"a[1]"."a[2]; ok=1; exit} END{exit !ok}'; } || { command -v dpkg-query >/dev/null 2>&1 && ver="$(dpkg-query -W -f="${Version}\n" rocm-core 2>/dev/null)" && [ -n "$ver" ] && awk -F'[.-]' '{print "rocm"$1"."$2; exit}' <<<"$ver"; } || { command -v rpm >/dev/null 2>&1 && ver="$(rpm -q --qf '%{VERSION}\n' rocm-core 2>/dev/null)" && [ -n "$ver" ] && awk -F'[.-]' '{print "rocm"$1"."$2; exit}' <<<"$ver"; })"; [ -n "$ROCM_TAG" ] && uv pip install "torch>=2.4,<2.11.0" "torchvision<0.26.0" "torchaudio<2.11.0" --index-url "https://download.pytorch.org/whl/$ROCM_TAG" --upgrade --force-reinstall ``` *Note: If your ROCm version is 7.2 or higher, replace `$ROCM_TAG` in the command above with `rocm7.1,` no PyTorch wheels exist yet for 7.2+.*
{% endstep %} {% step %} #### **Install Unsloth** Install Unsloth with AMD extras: {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```bash uv pip install unsloth[amd] ``` {% endcode %}
⚠️ Required for AMD: install ROCm-compatible bitsandbytes\ All ROCm systems need a pre-release bitsandbytes build, versions ≤ 0.49.2 have a 4-bit decode NaN bug on every AMD GPU. Note: use `pip` not `uv` for this step, `uv` rejects the pre-release wheel due to a version mismatch in the filename. {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```bash # x86_64 systems: pip install --force-reinstall --no-cache-dir --no-deps \ "https://github.com/bitsandbytes-foundation/bitsandbytes/releases/download/continuous-release_main/bitsandbytes-1.33.7.preview-py3-none-manylinux_2_24_x86_64.whl" # aarch64 systems: replace x86_64 with aarch64 in the URL above # Fallback if the URL is unreachable: # pip install --force-reinstall --no-cache-dir --no-deps "bitsandbytes>=0.49.1" ``` {% endcode %}
{% endstep %} {% step %} #### **Start fine-tuning with Unsloth!** And that's it. Try some examples in our [**Unsloth Notebooks**](/docs/get-started/unsloth-notebooks.md) page! You can view our dedicated [fine-tuning](/docs/get-started/fine-tuning-llms-guide.md) or [reinforcement learning](/docs/get-started/reinforcement-learning-rl-guide.md) guides. Heres a brief example as well: **1. Set environment variables** {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```bash export HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=9.4.2 # Required for AMD MI300X export HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1 # Fixes HuggingFace download issues on AMD ``` {% endcode %} ***Note:*** *`HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=9.4.2` tells ROCm to treat your GPU as gfx942 (MI300X). Without this, some kernels may fail to compile or run.* **2. Load and configure model** {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```python from unsloth import FastModel model, tokenizer = FastModel.from_pretrained( model_name = "unsloth/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it", max_seq_length = 2048, load_in_4bit = True, ) model = FastModel.get_peft_model( model, r = 16, lora_alpha = 16, target_modules = ["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], ) ``` {% endcode %} **3. Train** {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```python from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig trainer = SFTTrainer( model = model, tokenizer = tokenizer, train_dataset = dataset, formatting_func = formatting_func, args = SFTConfig( per_device_train_batch_size = 1, gradient_accumulation_steps = 4, max_steps = 60, output_dir = "outputs", report_to = "none", ), ) trainer_stats = trainer.train() ``` {% endcode %}
***Note:** On AMD GPUs, Flash Attention 2 is not available. Unsloth automatically falls back to Xformers, which provides equivalent performance on ROCm. The warning can be safely ignored.* {% endstep %} {% endstepper %} ### :1234: Reinforcement Learning on AMD GPUs You can use our :ledger:[gpt-oss RL auto win 2048](https://github.com/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-gpt_oss_\(20B\)_Reinforcement_Learning_2048_Game_BF16.ipynb) example on a MI300X (192GB) GPU. The goal is to play the 2048 game automatically and win it with RL. The LLM (gpt-oss 20b) auto devises a strategy to win the 2048 game, and we calculate a high reward for winning strategies, and low rewards for failing strategies. {% columns %} {% column %} {% endcolumn %} {% column %} The reward over time is increasing after around 300 steps or so! The goal for RL is to maximize the average reward to win the 2048 game. {% endcolumn %} {% endcolumns %} We used an AMD MI300X machine (192GB) to run the 2048 RL example with Unsloth, and it worked well!
You can also use our :ledger:[automatic kernel gen RL notebook](https://github.com/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-gpt_oss_\(20B\)_GRPO_BF16.ipynb) also with gpt-oss to auto create matrix multiplication kernels in Python. The notebook also devices multiple methods to counteract reward hacking. {% columns %} {% column width="50%" %} The prompt we used to auto create these kernels was: {% code overflow="wrap" %} ```` Create a new fast matrix multiplication function using only native Python code. You are given a list of list of numbers. Output your new function in backticks using the format below: ``` python def matmul(A, B): return ... ``` ```` {% endcode %} {% endcolumn %} {% column width="50%" %} The RL process learns for example how to apply the Strassen algorithm for faster matrix multiplication inside of Python. {% endcolumn %} {% endcolumns %} ### :books:AMD Free One-click notebooks AMD provides one-click notebooks equipped with **free 192GB VRAM MI300X GPUs** through their Dev Cloud. Train large models completely for free (no signup or credit card required): * [Qwen3 (32B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Qwen3_\(32B\)_A100-Reasoning-Conversational.ipynb) * [Llama 3.3 (70B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-Llama3.3_\(70B\)_A100-Conversational.ipynb) * [Qwen3 (14B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-Qwen3_\(14B\)-Reasoning-Conversational.ipynb) * [Mistral v0.3 (7B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-Mistral_v0.3_\(7B\)-Alpaca.ipynb) * [GPT OSS MXFP4 (20B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/AMD-GPT_OSS_MXFP4_\(20B\)-Inference.ipynb) - Inference * [Gemma4 (E2B)](https://amd-ai-academy.com/github/unslothai/notebooks/blob/main/nb/Gemma4_\(E2B\)_Reinforcement_Learning_Sudoku_Game.ipynb) - RL Sudoku * Unsloth Studio {% embed url="" %} You can use any Unsloth notebook by prepending in [Unsloth Notebooks](/docs/get-started/unsloth-notebooks.md) by changing the link from \ to {% columns %} {% column width="33.33333333333333%" %} {% endcolumn %} {% column width="66.66666666666667%" %} {% endcolumn %} {% endcolumns %} --- # Agent Instructions: Querying This Documentation If you need additional information that is not directly available in this page, you can query the documentation dynamically by asking a question. Perform an HTTP GET request on the current page URL with the `ask` query parameter: ``` GET https://unsloth.ai/docs/get-started/install/amd.md?ask= ``` The question should be specific, self-contained, and written in natural language. The response will contain a direct answer to the question and relevant excerpts and sources from the documentation. Use this mechanism when the answer is not explicitly present in the current page, you need clarification or additional context, or you want to retrieve related documentation sections.
This is the key! The **pre-release bitsandbytes** fixes the segfault on AMD. Let me install it and build the working script.

messages.content: Same segfault at the same place — it's the bitsandbytes 4-bit weight dequantization kernel crashing on gfx1032. Let me try a completely different approach: **skip 4-bit quantization** and use fp16 directly with aggressive memory optimization. granite-4.1-3b is 6.81 GB in fp16, leaving 1.76 GB free. With `num_generations=2` and short completions + gradient checkpointing, this might work.

function.arguments:
{"command":"cd /home/alex/Projects/portkit/scripts && . .venv_grpo/bin/activate && HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION=10.3.0 HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET=1 python -c "
import os
os.environ['HSA_OVERRIDE_GFX_VERSION'] = '10.3.0'
os.environ['HF_HUB_DISABLE_XET'] = '1'
import torch
import gc

# Test: fp16 model + LoRA + generation memory footprint
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b',
    dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map='cuda',
    trust_remote_code=True,
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('ibm-granite/granite-4.1-3b', trust_remote_code=True)
if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

print(f'After model load: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')

# Apply LoRA
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=4,  # Very small rank
    lora_alpha=8,
    target_modules=['q_proj', 'v_proj'],
    lora_dropout=0.0,
    bias='none',
    task_type='CAUSAL_LM',
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
print(f'After LoRA: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')

# Test generation with short sequence
inputs = tokenizer('Hello world', return_tensors='pt').to('cuda')
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=64, do_sample=True, temperature=0.8, pad_token_id=tokenizer.pad_token_id)
print(f'After gen (64 tok): {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
print(f'Max mem: {torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats()

# Test forward pass with gradient
model.train()
inputs = tokenizer('Test input for gradient computation', return_tensors='pt').to('cuda')
with torch.cuda.amp.autocast(dtype=torch.float16):
    out = model(**inputs, labels=inputs['input_ids'])
    out.loss.backward()
print(f'After fwd+bwd: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
print(f'Peak: {torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB')
" 2>&1","description":"Test fp16+LoRA memory footprint for GRPO","timeout":300}
function.name: bash

messages.content:
Excellent! This is very promising:
- Model load: **6.81 GB**
- After LoRA: **6.81 GB** (LoRA adds only ~5 MB)
- After generation (64 tokens): **6.84 GB**, peak **6.85 GB**
- After forward + backward: **6.88 GB**, peak **6.91 GB**

That leaves ~1.7 GB headroom! With gradient checkpointing enabled (which trades compute for memory), this should work for GRPO. Let me now test the actual GRPO setup with `num_generations=2`:

messages.content:
`epsilon_high` defaults to `epsilon` (0.2) when `None`. So the previous crash was only happening with the Unsloth-compiled version. With standard trl GRPOTrainer (not compiled), `epsilon_high=None` gets resolved to 0.2 at init. Good — no fix needed for the standard path.

Now let me check what `bf16` does on AMD — RDNA2 doesn't support bf16 natively:
